ideas: Be patient and understanding about nausea, fatigue, and mood changes — they are caused by powerful hormonal shifts.
Help with cooking smells and food prep, since strong odours can trigger nausea right now.
Keep simple snacks like crackers or fruit within easy reach for queasy moments.
Take on more of the household chores so your partner can rest when she needs to.
Attend the first prenatal visit or scan together if you can, and learn the due date together.
Encourage and gently remind about daily folic acid and prenatal vitamins.
Celebrate milestones together, like feeling the first movements and the anatomy scan.
Go for gentle walks together to support energy, mood, and connection.
Help plan and prepare balanced, nutritious meals rich in iron, calcium, and protein.
Start talking and reading to the bump — the baby can begin to hear your voice.
Begin researching and planning for the baby's arrival, like the nursery and essentials.
Offer back rubs and help with comfort as the bump grows and aches appear.
Help create a comfortable sleep setup with extra pillows for side-sleeping.
Take on heavier tasks and lifting so she can rest and avoid strain.
Attend prenatal classes together to learn about labour, birth, and newborn care.
Practise relaxation and breathing techniques together for use during labour.
Help pack the hospital bag and keep important documents and contacts ready.
Learn the signs of labour and the plan for getting to the hospital so you both feel prepared.
Confirm the route to the hospital, transport plan, and emergency contacts in advance.
Keep the hospital bag, phone charger, and paperwork ready by the door.
Be a calm, reassuring presence and a steady advocate during labour.
Support her chosen birth preferences while staying flexible if plans need to change.
Arrange help at home for the first days after birth, like meals and cleaning.
Plan to share newborn care such as nappy changes, soothing, and night-time support so rest can be shared.
